beats.headline: One old password darkens a fuel pipeline.
beats.body: Attackers signed into a forgotten VPN account that had no second factor, and a pipeline serving much of the U.S. East Coast went offline. Not a zero-day — just one unguarded door.
say: Every gap, every shortcut, every forgotten door — I will find it.
Not tonight. One stolen login should never be enough to end a city.
Where every attacker does — at the wall.

beats.title: AEGIS CITADEL — NIGHTFALL
say: Every fortress has a door. I only need one careless soul to leave one open.
Then meet the three guardians of the gate. I'm Vega. This is the apprentice, Nova.

beats.title: THE OUTER WALLS
beats.subtitle: Access Control · Identity · Physical
say: Layer one is the perimeter, who gets in, and what they may touch.
Three families stand the wall: Access Control, Identification, and Physical.

beats.title: THE CITADEL MAP
say: Three districts light up the map: AC, IA, and PE.

beats.protects: Who may enter the city, and what they may touch.
beats.reality: Policies and mechanisms that decide which users and processes may use which systems and data.
say: It decides who may enter, and exactly what they may touch.
In the real world, that's the policies and mechanisms deciding which users and processes may use which systems and data.

beats.title: Account Management
beats.plain: Create, manage, review, and remove accounts across their whole lifecycle.
beats.why: Stale or rogue accounts are a favorite way in; managing them closes that door.
say: With accounts. AC-2 governs every account from birth to deletion.

beats.title: Least Privilege
beats.plain: Give every user and process only the access they truly need — nothing more.
beats.why: If an account is stolen, least privilege limits how far the intruder can reach.
say: Then the rule that saves you again and again, AC-6, Least Privilege.
Lock them all down. I only need the one account you forgot to trim.

beats.quote: Employ the principle of least privilege, allowing only authorized accesses for users (or processes acting on behalf of users) that are necessary to accomplish assigned organizational tasks.
say: Employ the principle of least privilege, allowing only authorized accesses for users (or processes acting on behalf of users) that are necessary to accomplish assigned organizational tasks.

beats.title: How the gate decides
say: Every request runs the same path: a policy decides, and a gate enforces it.
Say Alice works in payroll. Policy lets her open payroll records; the same gate blocks Bob from marketing.
So the policy decides... and something makes the gate obey — is that AC-3?

beats.title: Access Control
bullets: AC-2 Account Management — manage the full account lifecycle
AC-3 Access Enforcement — enforce approved authorizations
AC-6 Least Privilege — grant only the minimum access needed

beats.oath: None pass unknown; none hold more than they need.
say: Say the Gatekeeper's oath with me. None pass unknown; none hold more than they need.

beats.protects: Proves you are who you claim, before you pass.
beats.reality: Verifying the identity of users, devices, and processes before granting access.
say: Next, the Seal-Bearer, Identification and Authentication.
So Access Control checks the rules — but this one makes you prove who you are?

beats.title: Identification and Authentication (Organizational Users)
beats.plain: Uniquely identify and authenticate organizational users before granting access.
beats.why: It's the difference between 'someone' and a known, accountable person.
say: IA-2 demands proof of identity for every organizational user.
Its enhancements — like IA-2(1) and IA-2(2) — are where multi-factor authentication lives.

beats.expand: Something you know + something you have + something you are
beats.plain: Proving who you are with two or more different kinds of evidence — so one stolen password isn't enough to get in.
beats.example: A password (know) plus a one-time code from your phone (have) — or a fingerprint (are).
say: Multi-factor authentication — what is that, exactly?
Two kinds of proof instead of one. It's why the pipeline breach we opened with would have failed.

beats.title: Authenticator Management
beats.plain: Manage authenticators — passwords, tokens, certificates — across their lifecycle.
beats.why: Weak or leaked credentials are a top breach vector; this control hardens them.
say: And the passwords and tokens themselves?
IA-5 manages them, strength, rotation, protection.

beats.quote: Uniquely identify and authenticate organizational users and associate that unique identification with processes acting on behalf of those users.
beats.cite: NIST SP 800-53r5  ·  IA-2 Identification and Authentication (Organizational Users)
say: Uniquely identify and authenticate organizational users and associate that unique identification with processes acting on behalf of those users.

beats.title: Identification and Authentication
bullets: IA-2 Identification and Authentication (Organizational Users) — prove who organizational users are (MFA via enhancements)
IA-5 Authenticator Management — manage passwords, tokens, and certificates
IA-8 Identification and Authentication (Non-organizational Users) — authenticate non-organizational users too

beats.oath: Prove the name, or the gate stays shut.
say: The Seal-Bearer's oath. Prove the name, or the gate stays shut.

beats.protects: Holds the physical gates, and guards against fire and flood.
beats.reality: Physical access controls plus protection from hazards like fire, power loss, and water.
say: Last on the wall, the Wall-Warden, Physical and Environmental Protection.
Why pick a lock... when I can walk through an open door?
In reality, PE is physical access control — guards, badges, locks — plus protection from hazards like fire, power loss, and water.

beats.title: Physical Access Control
beats.plain: Enforce who may physically enter — with guards, badges, locks, and logs.
beats.why: Code can't stop a person who strolls into the server room; PE can.
say: PE-3 enforces physical access at the doors themselves.
Tailgating, propped doors, lost badges — so this one's literally about the building?

beats.title: Monitoring Physical Access
beats.plain: Monitor and review physical access to facilities.
beats.why: Watching the doors turns a break-in into an alarm, not a mystery.
say: And PE-6 watches and logs who comes and goes.

beats.quote: Enforce physical access authorizations at [entry and exit points] by: Verifying individual access authorizations before granting access to the facility;
beats.cite: NIST SP 800-53r5  ·  PE-3 Physical Access Control
say: Enforce physical access authorizations at [entry and exit points] by: Verifying individual access authorizations before granting access to the facility;

beats.title: Physical and Environmental Protection
bullets: PE-2 Physical Access Authorizations — authorize who may enter facilities
PE-3 Physical Access Control — enforce entry at the doors
PE-6 Monitoring Physical Access — monitor and log physical access

beats.oath: A door is a decision — and I decide.
say: And the Wall-Warden's oath. A door is a decision — and I decide.

beats.title: The Outer Walls in the RMF
bullets: These families are SELECTED from a 800-53B baseline for your system
Then IMPLEMENTED as technical and physical controls
And ASSESSED and MONITORED by the High Council (CA, later in the series)
say: Remember the framework? The wall is where you implement what you selected.
Select from the baseline, implement here, assess later.

beats.q: Which guardian proves you are who you claim, before you pass?
say: Guardian check. Who proves your identity?
beats.why: Identification and Authentication proves who you are before you're let in.

beats.q: 'Only the access you truly need' is which control?
say: And the rule that limits the blast radius?
beats.why: AC-6, Least Privilege, grants only the access someone truly needs — nothing more.

beats.q: An intruder tailgates through a propped door into the server room. Which family should stop it?
say: Scenario: someone tailgates into the server room. Whose watch is that?
beats.why: Physical and Environmental Protection stops tailgating into the room itself.

beats.title: Episode 01 — the Outer Walls
lines: AC decides who may do what (AC-2, AC-3, AC-6).
IA proves the name is real before you pass — MFA lives in IA-2's enhancements.
PE guards the physical doors and environment (PE-2, PE-3, PE-6).
beats.mnemonic: Right person, right door, right reason.
say: Notebook: the wall is access, identity, and physical — working together.
Three guardians, one wall. The perimeter holds.
Your gates hold — for now. So I'll stop knocking, and start hiding.
Then we'll need the watchtowers. Next time.
